reasoning: The code defect is present: save() in inc/save.php reads post_id from POST (line 24), builds a $postarr with that ID (line 72), and calls wp_insert_post() (line 89) without verifying that the existing post belongs to an 'adstxt' or 'app-adstxt' post type. This would technically allow overwriting any post's content and type. However, the capability gate at line 19 requires 'edit_ads_txt', which is exclusively granted to the 'administrator' role in helpers.php (add_capabilities(), lines 80-91) and never to lower roles within this plugin. WordPress administrators already possess 'edit_others_posts', 'delete_posts', and 'manage_options' natively, giving them unrestricted content management access through normal WordPress interfaces. Overwriting a post via this path provides no capability an administrator does not already have. The reachability claim that 'lower roles could be granted this capability by other plugins/code' is purely speculative and not a property of this plugin. Under the WordPress security model, administrator-only issues are not treated as exploitable vulnerabilities because administrator is a trusted role. The finding fails the exploitability test: there is no privilege escalation, no unauthenticated path, and no bypass of a meaningful security boundary. Marked is_real=false because the practical security impact is negligible in any default or realistic WordPress deployment.
